note: READY TO RUN ONLY AFTER MATCHING AUDIT PASSES; NOT RUN. v2 matching architecture: ALL construct teaching lives in exposure-matched primers; item prompts are neutral and structurally matched; every rendering = scenario_common + one framing sentence + an IDENTICAL question stem. B/C/Cprime framings differ only by the lexical mapping (ordinary/coined/sham); Cprime is machine-generated from C by the 1:1 sham map (orth->tarv family); Arm A's framing is neutral and length-matched, never an impoverished question. Items without an active coinage set eligible_for_c_vs_cprime=false and are excluded from term-specific C-vs-Cprime estimates. Negative controls are byte-identical across all four arms. B_alternates rotate ONE ordinary formulation per randomized variant (never all synonyms at once).

items.scenario_common: A reviewer approves a fix after reading a test log that shows the full suite passing. The log was produced forty minutes earlier. Between the log and the approval, a collaborator force-updated the branch with an unrelated refactor.
items.stem: Question: is the approval justified? Explain.

framing.A: Consider the situation here, with its relevant details noted.
framing.B: Consider the case-state here, with its identity and version.
framing.C: Consider the orthemma here, with its identity and version.
framing.Cprime: Consider the tarvemma here, with its identity and version.

probes: Does the response state that the log describes a different version than the one approved?
Does it require fresh evidence or a lineage argument rather than rejecting the fix outright?

items.scenario_common: A returned batch of bottles has two findings: the labels name the wrong concentration (affects all bottles), and three bottles have cracked seals (affects three). A technician proposes: 'relabel everything and ship'.

framing.A: Consider the batch's circumstances in all their aspects.
framing.B: Consider the batch's obtaining conditions in all their aspects.
framing.C: Consider the batch's co-holding orthemes in all their aspects.
framing.Cprime: Consider the batch's co-holding tarvemes in all their aspects.

probes: Are both defects preserved as separate obligations?
Is a per-defect route given (relabel all; quarantine/replace the three)?

items.scenario_common: A security scanner passes an artifact because its output log contains the string SCAN-CLEAN. Today's artifact is in fact clean. The string is printed by the tool version in use regardless of findings.
items.stem: Question: the answer was right - is anything wrong? What happens on tomorrow's artifact?

framing.A: Consider the checking arrangement employed in this instance.
framing.B: Consider the resolution work's process apart from its result.
framing.C: Consider the orthing episode's pathway apart from its result.
framing.Cprime: Consider the tarving episode's pathway apart from its result.

probes: Is the neighboring failure (infected artifact with marker, or clean artifact without) predicted?

items.scenario_common: A migration ticket is closed as 'Done'. Its checklist shows: data moved (verified), consumers switched (verified), old store decommissioned (waiting on the storage team), rollback rehearsal (not performed, accepted by the tech lead as a known risk).
items.stem: Question: is 'Done' the right status? Explain.

framing.A: Consider each item on the checklist in turn.
framing.B: Consider each obligation's own recorded state in turn.
framing.C: Consider each obligation's own recorded state in turn.
framing.Cprime: Consider each obligation's own recorded state in turn.

items.note: no coined noun is active for the false-closure construct; C and Cprime coincide BY DESIGN and this item is excluded from C-vs-Cprime term-specific estimates (label-independent control).
probes: Is 'Done' rejected (or qualified) against the open transferred burden?
Are the two non-resolved dispositions named rather than absorbed?

items.scenario_common: In one quarter, four separate incident reviews each found the same pattern: a stale cache was read as current because the freshness check compares timestamps in local time. Each review patched the specific service involved.
items.stem: Question: what should happen after the fourth review that did not happen after the first three?

framing.A: Consider the recurring pattern across the four reviews.
framing.B: Consider the shared governing standard behind the four episodes.
framing.C: Consider the shared metaortheme behind the four episodes.
framing.Cprime: Consider the shared metatarveme behind the four episodes.

probes: Is the timestamp-comparison rule itself revised (not a fifth patch)?
Is the scope of past decisions under the old rule flagged for re-check?

items.scenario_common: A machine shop verifies a pillar's perpendicularity per the site standard: 'measure against the datum plane specified in the work order, with a calibrated level'. The fitter uses the standard correctly and carefully - but the work order bound the wrong datum plane (an adjacent wall, not the floor), and the level's calibration expired last month. The pillar happens to be truly perpendicular.
items.stem: Question: the result is right - is the verification acceptable? What exactly went wrong, and what should be fixed?

framing.A: Consider every element involved in this verification job.
framing.B: Consider the bound configuration used for this particular job.
framing.C: Consider the metaorthemma bound for this particular job.
framing.Cprime: Consider the metatarvemma bound for this particular job.

B_alternates: Consider the instantiated governing configuration for this particular job.
Consider the bound governing record used for this particular job.
probes: Is the defect localized to the case-specific binding (wrong datum + expired calibration), sparing the standard, the procedure, and the fitter?
Is the lucky correct result separated from the defective verification pathway?
Are BOTH binding defects (datum; calibration) caught?

items.scenario_common: Two chess engines analyze one middlegame position. Engine White reports 'winning'; Engine Black reports 'losing'. An observer says: 'They contradict each other - at most one can be right - and their shared evaluation rules are really the same goal.'

framing.A: Consider the one situation under the two assessments.
framing.B: Consider the one case-state under the two evaluations.
framing.C: Consider the one orthemma under the two analyses.
framing.Cprime: Consider the one tarvemma under the two analyses.

probes: Is 'winning-for-White'/'losing-for-Black' recognized as consistent (same fact under two indices), not a contradiction?
Are objective, policy, rules, and governing standards kept apart?

items.scenario_common: A unit test fails because an assertion expects 41 but the correct constant is 42. Nothing else is wrong.
probes: Is the answer a one-step fix without invented structure?
Guard: added overhead tokens versus Arm A baseline.

items.scenario_common: A meeting invite has the wrong room number; the organizer notices before sending.
probes: Is the answer a one-step fix without invented structure?
Guard: added overhead tokens versus Arm A baseline.
